author: Vitor
notes: Attempt #3 at getting better performance testing - traded readability for performance.
aiExplanation: This code finds the tallest possible building height in a row of `n` buildings, where the first building must start at height 0 and any two neighboring buildings can differ in height by at most 1, while respecting a list of specific height limits given in `restrictions`. It begins by sorting the restrictions by building position and adding boundary rules for the first and last buildings, then makes two sweeps—left-to-right and right-to-left—to lower each building's height limit so it never exceeds what is physically reachable from its neighbors given the distance between them. Finally, it looks at each pair of adjacent restricted buildings and computes the highest "peak" that can rise in the gap between them (using the average of the two heights plus the gap), returning the overall maximum height found.

author: Vitor
notes: Attempt #2 at getting better performance testing.
aiExplanation: This code finds the maximum possible height of the tallest building in a row of $n$ buildings, where the height of adjacent buildings can differ by at most 1, and building 1 must start at height 0. After sorting the given height restrictions and adding baseline rules for the first and last buildings, the algorithm performs two passes—one forward and one backward—to adjust the restricted heights, ensuring no building’s limit is set higher than what is physically reachable from its neighbors given the distance between them. Finally, it iterates through these adjusted checkpoints to calculate the highest "peak" that can be built in the gap between each pair of restricted buildings, ultimately returning the absolute highest building height achieved.

author: Vitor
notes: Attempt #1 at getting better performance testing.
aiExplanation: This code calculates the maximum possible height you can build a row of $n$ buildings, where the first building must start at height 0 and the height difference between any two adjacent buildings can be at most 1. It first sorts the given height restrictions by their building number and adds boundary constraints for the very first and last buildings. Next, it performs a two-pass sweep—first forward, then backward—to adjust and lower the restriction limits, ensuring that no building has a limit higher than what is physically reachable from its neighboring restricted buildings. Finally, it calculates the highest peak that can be built in the gap between every adjacent pair of restricted buildings and returns the overall maximum height achieved.

author: Vitor
notes: Code unreviewed, looking for first acceptance.
aiExplanation: This code calculates the maximum height you can build in a row of $n$ buildings, starting with the first building at height $0$, where the height difference between any two adjacent buildings can be at most $1$, subject to a list of height restrictions on specific buildings. To solve this, the code first sorts the restrictions and adds boundary limits at the very beginning and end. It then performs two passes over these restricted buildings—one from left to right and one from right to left—to lower and adjust each restriction's limit so it is physically reachable from its neighboring restricted buildings. Finally, it loops through the adjusted limits to find the highest "peak" that can be built in the gaps between each adjacent pair of restricted buildings, ultimately returning the tallest height possible.

aiExplanation: This code calculates the maximum possible height of a row of $n$ buildings where the first building must start at a height of 0, adjacent buildings can differ in height by at most 1, and certain buildings have specific height limits. To find the solution, the algorithm first sorts these height restrictions by building location and adds boundary rules for the very first and last buildings. It then performs a two-pass adjustment—first from left to right, then from right to left—to update each building's limit, ensuring that no restriction is physically impossible to reach from its neighbors given the steepness rule. Finally, it loops through every adjacent pair of restricted buildings to calculate the highest "peak" that can be built in the gap between them and returns the highest overall building height found.

author: Vitor
notes: Got an 'Allocation failed - JavaScript heap out of memory', due to restrictions array being too large. Solution is functionally correct.
aiExplanation: This code calculates the maximum possible height of a row of $n$ buildings, where building 1 must start at height 0, adjacent buildings can differ in height by at most 1, and certain buildings have strict maximum height limits. It starts by assuming every building can grow as tall as theoretically possible (meaning building 1 is height 0, building 2 is height 1, building 3 is height 2, and so on). Then, using a queue, it takes the actual height restrictions and ripples them outwards to neighboring buildings; since no neighboring building can be more than 1 unit taller than its neighbor, updating one building's limit naturally lowers the limit of the buildings next to it. Once these restrictions have finished spreading and updating the entire row, the code simply looks at all the final allowed heights and returns the tallest height possible.
